neen’sFitive na Mdise, and a prize essay by one of the
" students, Mr. Nicholas M‘Nally, deals with Brian Boru
- and his times. The subjects of several of the debates

show the effects of the Irish Ireland influence—for fn-

and Flood. Bubt it is really the Diary that

Wo rofer to the programme of tho St. Patrick’s night
entortainment, and we find tha! in the concert part of
it, there was not one song in Irich. What is the use of
having a peep at the Limerick procession if there is
not oven one Irish song on the programme of thz con-
cor at the College in the night?

There appears to have been five entertainments dur-
ing the year, but there was not as much as one Irish
play. Is it not a pity that Mungret should be so very
far behind Maynooth College in the matter of Irish
drama? In the course of “Our Past’’ we find that
one of the pastmen, Father M. Kenny, S.J., writing
from Augusta, Ga., refers to meeting Father Coyle at
Birmingham, Ala., who took him twenty-five miles by
electric car to Bessemer, where they met Father John
Kelly. Father John Kelly is evidently a most excel-
lent man, for in the course of Father Kenny’s letter in
the Mungre: Annual we read:—“We three talked
Mungret, of course, Father Kelly occasionally varying
tho discourse by recitals from the Leaper.”  The
Mungre: Annual is very neatly brought out by Messrs.
Guy and Co., of Limerick, the illustrations are various,
and many of them particularly interesting. Perhaps
the next issue will contain an account of a play in Irish,

The Jaanuary number of the Irish Rosary has the
marks of progress and enterprise stamped upon it. It is
moving with the times, and looks also as if it intended
to make the times move some of the way with it.
Several new features are introduced, and in discarding
the two columns to the page, and going back to the
original undivided page, we think it has done well.
The type, too, is somewhat larger, and that also is an
improvemont.  The January number leads off with
Editorial Notes, and the first article is by Father
Dinneen, who discusses in interesting fashion * The
Gaelic League and Non-Sectarianism.”” This is a sub-
ject of great importance—it might conceivably be one
of vital importance—to the League. Father Dinneen,
like ourselves, is not in love with the term ‘ non-sec-
tarian,”’ which, applied %o mixed bodies containing
Catholics, seems to imply that the Church is only e
“sect.” = He would prefer the term “ non-denomina- |
tional.”” However, that is only by the way. <

Father Dinneen’s article is well worth not only read-

Gaelic League. Weo are in entire accord with him in
the importance which ho attaches to the League stick-
ing to the main objects and keeping the various second-
ary matters which fringe round it in their proper sub-
ordinate places.

Ho very portinently remarks:—“The power the
League can wield in the country will, I believe, be .
direotly proportionate to the energy it devotes to the
attainment of its main objects. When a society sets
itself to do a certain work of vast extent and National
importance, a great many persons will hesitate to join
it or help its work, no matter how much they may be
in sympathy with that work, until they see how it is
likely to succeed. It is not by dividing its attention
on a multiplicity of objects, but by concentrating its
energies on a few main objects that it is likely )
hold of the popular imagination and enlist on its side
the pusillanimous, the wavering, the dubious, the re-
luctant. Nothing is more calculated U
indifferent and prevent them fro
cause with society than a tende:
indefinitely tho sphere of ]

302

l}hemfore, that the more distinctly the League keeps
its main objects before the public, and the more
strenuously it labours for these objects, only bestowing
_secondary consideration on its secondary objects, the
more sure}y will it attain its main objects, and the
greater will be the support it will receive from all
classes of the community. For several of its minor
objects there are special societies already in existence.
Lot these societies promote theso objects to the best of
the\u-_a.blliby. The main objects of the League are of
sufficient weight and importanco to ongross tho main
stream of its energy. If I were to sce the Leaguo
10§111g ground on the question of preserving vernacular
Irish, even though I saw it labour successfully in tho
promotion of, say, native dances or home industries, I
would begin to . lose faith in it. I would begin to
doubt the soundness of its constitution or the pro-
bability of its ultimate success.”

One reason why politics took the placo of Nation-
ality in recent times was that it is so casy to bo a
politician. If you are not a “spouter’’ you can be a
politician by merely being a shouter. The Revival of
the Language is a heavy piece of work to undertako,
and, like a big business undertaking, it is mostly made
up of plodding, prosy work. Anyone could foresee that
a great many peoplo attracted to the Irish language re-
vival would get tired after a while, and pine for more
“ heroic ”’ work—work giving greater opportunities for
strutting on the public stage. In the League the
conditions tend towards exacting the maximum amount
of work and a minimum amount of show; present-day
Irish character thirsts rather for a maximum amount
of show and a minimum amount of work. The latter
was the disease from which in recent years politics in
Ireland suffered; and pouring the old character into
new bottles won’t necessarily change the character,
though it may considerably influence it. We are not
surprised that the Language revival proper should
prove wearisome to many, and that they should mani-
fest a disposition to branch out into more showy occu-
pations. That development was to be expected, and
the Gaelic League should be consciously on its guard
against it. Father Dinneen touches on this side of the
question in his article, but, of course, the article is
mainly concerned with the Leagus as an organisation
in relation to the established religious denominations
in this country.

Amongst the other arbicles in the January number
of the Irish Rosary is a “spicy *’ review of “ Mike, the
Catholic’s >’ latest book. ‘Mike”’ has turned his at-
tention to England now, and has written a book—* The
Church and State in England and Wales.”” ‘We hope
all the old Sourfaces who bought Mike’s books about
Treland will like his latest work. The review of it in
the Irish Rosary quotes the following from an article
in the so-called Church of Ireland Gazette:—If the
authorities of the Roman Church had imposed upon
Mr, M‘Carthy, as a Roman Catholic, the task, as a
penance for his sins in writing his two former books,
to do something to injure the cause of Protestantism,
he could not have carried out their behests more effec-
tively than he has done by writing this book.” Per-
haps the Sourfaces would now send the “hat’’ round
for another testimonial for “ Mike !

‘What has come over Ballyvourney, in the County of
Cork? At one time it was “the Capital of Ircland,”
but wo seldom hear anything about it now. In Mr.
William O’Brien’s paper of last week we seo headings—

. the wholo proceedings of the manifestation of “great

We wore not present, and depend on the Simply e

This gentleman told the meeting that *they

Mr. Lindsay Cirawford, the Unionist and “Indepen-

He further told his audience that they must choose
“either for democracy or for clericalism.”” And later

say which influence should be paramount: supposing
now that the Catholics were, by force of their numbers
and ability and the number of Catholic teachers, the
absorbing body !

THE POLICY OF ABSTENTION.

HE most striking item in the programme of the
quasi-extreme party in Ireland in our day is
the demand for a policy of abstention on the part of
the Irish representatives at Westminster. Those who
justly laugh at constitutional arguments based upon
the Renanciation Act of 1783, or who may think it
absurd to deny the reality of existing laws, as hard
facts that regulate the movements of policemen, may
yet recognise that the question of the advisability
of the Irish Party continuing their attendance
in Parliament is one to be soberly debated.
But in any such debate it must not be for-
gotten that hostility to Parliamentary action is
now founded upon different considerations to those
which inspired it in the old Fenian times. At an epoch
when men hoped to win independence, or at least a
frce constitution, by force of arms, with the same speed
and facility that, like blessings, were being obtained
in many of the countrics of Europe, it was not unreason-
able to look upon the speeches of Irish place-hunters as
an unprofitable distraction. To-day, however, the
aspirations of extremists have traversed the Alps and
the Leitha, chilled a little in the transit, it may be,
and Hungary, rather than Sicily or Venetia, is held
up to Irishmen as the example. And it follows, natur-
ally, that the policy of abstention in our day must be
supported by different arguments.

Now, what is the basis of this modern policy, a policy
which has never, perhaps, been entirely absent from
the mind of Gaelic Leaguers. It is not in our day
based upon considerations of lively expectation and
possible . distraction; it is, indeed, less the child of
hope than of despair. It is the product of two views:
that Parliamentary action involves a tremendous and
damaging admission against our National interests;
and that, with all this, it has signally failed to ad-
vance the cause of self-government. Accept these two
propositions in their entirety and it becomes almost
impossiblo to justify a policy of Parliamentary opposition
to an honest Nationalist. But are they to be accepted ?
As to the first, it is folly to shut our eyes to the fact
that Parliamentarianism does involve a most serious
admission. - To take a responsible part in and become
an integral factor of an assembly whose authority you
profess to repudiate, is to give away a great part of
your case. It is just as if Catholics were to attend a

-Protestant church through some ulterior pious motive.

Even the declaration “that we are here against our
will ”’ grows thinner and thinner with advancing years
boneath the trying glave of reality, when it is suffi- .
ciently apparent that you are not there against your
will, but have, in fact, spent £2 0s. 6d. on your ticket.

i
-

Still it must be remembered that for nigh a century
such an admission of authority was inevitable. 1885
was the first year in which a policy of abstention could
have been effectively carried out, and the yoear of tho
ﬁ}'s_b Home Rule Bill would not have beon a, very aus-
picious period in which to launch such a project.

. But the whole question really depends upon the con-
sideration whether in a policy of Parliamentary action
we in truth merely stoop to conquer; whether the ovil
we do our cause upon one side is counterbalanced by
gain upon the other. Now, in this matter in estimat-
ing the gain I confine myself to the gain accruing to
tthe cause of self-government. I put aside cmtirely Land
Acts, Town Tenants’ Bills, Labourers’ Cottages, and
the rest. All these measures are in their way good. But
they are little better than the shifts resorted to by a
man in difficulties to stave off inovitable bankruptcy.
For what Ireland needs is a profound rovolution. If
things continue as thoy are we must certainly die, for
all our shifts. Let matters take their course, let a
quarter of our population emigrate every twonty years,
in a cenfury Cromwell’s plan will have been accom-
piished, and there will be an Ireland without Irish-
men. In a case so desperate, no medicine can curo us,
save the medicine of life.

Can Parliamentary action, then, win for us this
right to life? This must be the touchstone of all pre-
tensions. In Parnell’s time it seemed sclf-evident that
it could; Home Rule was announced in the evening
papers. To-day men speak with less assurance; for tho
coming twelve months must decide all. They will tell
us whether, as the result of twenty years’ endurance, we
are any nearer our desires. And everything depends upon
the power of the House of Lords. Is it strong enough,
then, to resist the Liberal majority and stand between
the Irish people and their hope? Can it either reject
the Self-Government Bill or emasculate it till we are
as little ready to accept it as the Non-conformists were
to accept the Education Bill? If the Lords are strong
enough to resist or make ineffectual any concession of
self-government in the coming session of Parliament
and yet retain their power, Parliam:mtarianism
is clearly bankrupt. It has tried every kind
of attack, by a weak Government and by a strong one;
a large measure and a small one. It has failed abso-
lutely, as O’Connell’s policy failed at Clontarf, and it
only remains to foster an inane hope or else to try
something new.

But are the Lords strong enough to make our policy
bankrupt? I fear they are. They have routed the English
Non-conformists, a stronger body with a stronger man-
date than the Irish party. Why should they spare us?
Whatever we obtain must be by their gracs. For no-
thing is casier for them than calling devolution, Home
Rule, to deny its mandate. Of course they may de-
cide to let devolution through, as they let the Town
Tenants’ Bill. - If so the Irish Party will have gained
a substantial success, for they will have set the Union
upon the slippery slope, but that success is, to say the
least, highly problematical. :

Should the Party, on the contrary, fail, its policy
will have failed definitely, and we may reasonably
consider other methods of which a policy of abstention
will probably be rather the visible sign than the true
substance. For abstention will not create the situa-

tion ; it is rather the situation that will bring about
abstention. The truth is that if a new policy is re-
quired it has yet to bo devised, and present-day anti-
Parliamentarian policies express rather dissatisfaction
with what exists than a true conception of whab is to
be. And we must not forget that on the rare occasions

" when abstention by itself has been attempted in the Bri-

tish Parliament—by Fox, for instance—it has proved
a signal failure.

Looking at matters as a whole, the supreme moment;
has arrived, and Parliamentarianism must prove itsolf
once and for all or be for ever fallen; tho Irish Party
must givo account of its stewardship. The issue of a
fow months must decide, and, let us hope, even in the

AN UNRETURNED EMIGRANT.

HIS tells of Jerry Fox, the brother of Julia, who,
until he was fifty, sniffed with scornful amuse-
ment at the very mention of love—and then fell a
victim to it. It happened in this wise. Jerry came
over at twenty and became a London policeman. He
came because the ancestral acres were few and the sons
of his father many. But at heart he remained a man
of the soil. It was his passion. Through all the years
-of his ascent from constable to inspector he looked for-
ward to a pensioned return to the fields. He never
once lost his interest in all that belonged to farming.
Land questions, manures, cattle-rearing, dairying—in
all these things his interest remained deep and vivid,
‘though he applied himself to his work of maintaining
law and order in London with ardour. Perhaps, one
should say carnestness or steadfastness, rather than
ardour. For Jerry was not exactly ardent. He was a
plain, blunt man, not dull, but not mentally agile.
He had no ear for music, no eye for colour, no feeling
for poetry, was not subtle or complex.  There was
nothing sentimental about his desire for the ficlds of his
boyhood. He did not make mental pictures of them
under loved and beautiful aspects, and grow dim-eyed
and tender in the process. No, in a dogged way he was
quite determined to go back, but it was instinct more
than anything clse that moved him. Secking happi-
qness and a fitting environment, as we all do, con-
_sciously or unconsciously, there was deep down in him
-a certainty that on a farm at home was his proper
‘place. There was, too, something of a sense that his
place at home would be an honoured one. A litigious
people, fond of recourse to law and loving to dispute
about it, would surely look to him as a legal luminary.
The prospect minstered to his pride. e had no doubt
whateéver that he was a wan who should be looked up
“to. Thére was moréd than a touch of pompous self-
‘importance in Jerry. Ie had done very well in tho
force, getting his promotion honestly by dint of steady,
¢ondcientious serviéd. You cannot lord it over a lot
.of heavy-witted policeiien, mostly yokels, for many
years, and be rather fawnéd on by the humbler trades-
men, without coming to have a somewhat inflated
-opinion of yourself.  Jerry rather fancied himself,
wearing his top hat awkwatdly én the Sabbath, to be
sure, but with inéffable dirs of dignity and consequence.
A#id if he folt big in London, and was conscious of a
wealth of legal learning, what might he not look for-
ward to at home?, Anyway, home he meant to go, and
‘had meant, from tho beginning.
Now, that being so, it wad, of coursé, a very annoy-
er Julia refuse John Deasy’s

THE LEADER. 307

most opportune offer of marriage. She kept house for
her brother, but he knew right well that she dreaded
going home. And the people at home dreaded her
coming. They were a match for most critics who come
back full of harsh advice as to how things ought to be
done, but Julia was really a very sharp ‘‘ thorn in the
side,” and any community might well fight shy of her.
Yot if sho insancly persisted in refusing this mira-
culous chance, home she must go. As he said himself,
Jerry was mad to her. £

Now he had for long been friendly with the widow
of Sergeant Murphy. Since her bereavement she had
kept a trim little tobacco shop, and out of esprit de
corps the force all patronised her. She was a rounded
little woman of forty or so, smooth cheeked, sleek
haired, bright of eye and soft of speech, who had
worked up a thriving business by sheer good sense and
amiable manners.  Jorry was neither a hafer nor a
lover of women ; he simply did not bother about the sex
al all. But he got his tobacco always from Mrs.
Murphy, and rather liked a chat with her. Discussing
with her one day his coming retirement, he spoke with
some force of Julia’s obstinacy and the difficulty in
which it was likely to land him. The widow leant
with folded arms on the counter and looked up at him
strangely.  Her eyes had something compelling in
them.

“ Perhaps, she’s in love with someone else,’” she sug-
gested, and her voice was very soft and nice. Her
look and her tone were not lost in Jerry. But the
suggestion sounded madly ridiculous.

““In love!’’ he echoed. ‘‘Surely to God you don’t
think she’s taking leave of her senses? At her time of
life, too. What trash, to be sure!”’

The red of the widow’s cheeks deepened, but she
still looked in a way that, as it were, troubled him
agreeably.

‘“Oh, Mr. Fox, you're a terrible man,”' were her
words of rebuke. But somehow they sounded like a
verbal caress. They talked the matter over at length,
and, with strict injunctions to secrecy, she told him of
her and others’ suspicions as to Julia’s fondness for big
Donovan.  The widow had half-formed designs on
Jerry, and was delighted to have such a theme to
enlarge upon. For Jerry raged about the foolishness
of love in general, and the widow was love’s defender.
More than once she had, or took, occasion to say: “ Oh,
Mr. Fox, you're a terrible man!’’ sometimes accom-
panying the sweet rebuke with a tap on Jerry’s blue
sleeve.  And the burly inspector’s heavy face broke
into pleased smiles as she tapped him.

All the same he went home and raged at poor Julia,
with results that made for John Deasy’s happiness- in
the long run. Yet he went oftener than before to Mrs.
Murphy’s shop, ostensibly to talk of his sister’s pro-
spects, but really to cry out upon the folly of love, and
to hear that intoxicating ‘“ Oh, Mr. Fox, you're a
terrible man!”’

For a long time he did not understand what it all
meant, and whither it tended. For emotions were
strange to him, and he was not quick of mind. But
one night the blood seemed to rush to his head and
mako him dizzy. - His lips puckered in a curious way,
and moistened ; it was as if they had found a life and
will of their own, and wanted to drag him over the
counter towards that ripe pair on the other side. He
resisted them and steadied himself, and then went home
thinking deeply. The comether had been put on him
in earnest.

Then began a fight in the soul of Jerry Fox. He
wanted the widow; but he wanted the land as well, and
tho two together he could not have. Mrs. Murphy had
nover concealed her strong aversion from a return to
the country. It angered him to be pulled this way and.
that by conflicting desires, he who had always gone
straight along one plain way. He kept from the shop,
hut it was no use. FHe felt the familiar touch of her
fingers on his sleeve, her brown eyes haunted him, her
slow, soft “Oh, Mr. Fox, yow're a terrible man!”
made music in his ears. If all the love pootry of all

joéts were ¢ntrated in onme golden phrase it

would sound harsh to Jerry beside those oxquisite
words. 3

* At last, grown desperate, he roturnod and bluntly
offered marriage—on conditions. e had no cloquence,
but he made it clear to Mrs. Murphy that le wanted
her badly, and as badly wanted the two of them to go
home after the wedding. One small concession he
made ; if, as he know, the life of a farm was repugnant
to 1!01‘, they would take a house in the little town con-
venient to his old home. But on or nigh hand to the
land he must be. The widow was sweetness itself, but
she did not take his offer. “ I couldn’t, indeed I
couldn’t,”” she said carnestly. ‘I was reared on a
fi‘mn_. but I couldn’t go back to it. And as for any
dirty, footy little town—oh, I really couldn’t. The life
here suits me better than any other.” Jerry she would
accept: with a heart and a half, but only on condition
that he remained in London. A grieved and dis-
appointed man, he went off with a heavy tread and a
heavier heart.

Another period of internal strife ensued. The
woman and the land fought for him - within him.
It was a tough fight, but the woman won.
He went to the shop one night about closing
time, and, leaning over the counter, he took the
widow’s rosy face between his two hands and kissed it
with determination. This breach of decorum on the
part of her stolid lover really startled the widow. But
she knew what it meant. Pride of conquest, joy of
possession, shone in her eyes and thrilled in her tones
as she cried: “ Oh, Mr. Fox, you're a terrible man! ”’

FREE LIBRARIES.
11 O read or not to read,” is not the concrete

question before the public at present, as the

caption of “Imaal’s’’ article would lead one to believe.
The question is, what are people to read, and which are
the best agencies for supplying the people with suitable
reading? Is it Free Libraries, or Parochial Libraries?
This is the question that was debated, and decided
on, recently at the District Council meeting in New-
castle West, when, by a large majority, the motion in
favour of starting Free Libraries was rejected. As re-
gards books in the abstract, we aro all agreed, and
accept the dictum of “the Prince-of-Pagan’’ philoso-
phers, that books are the food of the mind, that is, what
material food is to the body, that, reading is to iho
mind. Now, as a person may take good, indifferent,
bad and hurtful food for his hody with corresponding
results to his corporal health; so the intellectual food
may be of various kinds, with corresponding resu'ts
likewise. One’s reading may be profitable, or indif-
ferent, or positively injurious to one’s intellectual aril
moral welfare. This nobody disputes. . The question
then is, which books are good, and which indifferent or
bad, and which medium is the better for disseminatiag
the good ones, and checking tho spread of the worthless
or gvil ones? This is the question of paramount impor-
tance at present. ‘Tmaal’’ tells us, “he does uot uow
read works of fiction.”” I have no doubt about it. If

THE SECRET OF SUCCESS.

HILOSOPHERS tell us that every agent has some
end in view when acting. This end is something
good in itself, or at least in its appearances. The intel-
lect apprehends it as good, and the will tends towards
its possession. It is thus that a free being acts. He
does not work like a machine, nor is he determined
towards one particular course; but he acts by election
because there is an end in view for which he is free to
strive. If he attains it, he experiences what is known
as satisfaction, and in case he does not, displeasure and
disappointment result.

Now cach of these two feelings or sensations react
upon his charaoter.  They increase or diminish
confidence, reverence or esteem of self, courage, and all
that we mean when we say the spirit of enterprise. The
impression they produce may depend upon many
things. It may depend upon the person’s natural
abilities, upon his training or education, upon the cir-
cumstances in which he is placed; but it principally
depends upon the degree of self-discipline to which he
has attained, and which enables one to bear, as far as
may be, with equanimity, the sorrow of defeat or the
joy of victory. Thus it is self-discipline which de-
lineates the character of the individual, and I may add
the character of the nation; because the multitude of
men and institutions of which a nation is composed is
itself made up of units, and it is with these we have to
deal in reforming the nation.. What is true of the in-
dividual is very often true of the class to which he be-
longs, and what is true of the class is also frequently
true of the nation. The individual, and the group
which constitute any society, may be considered in their
public and in their private capacities. In the former
there is something in common with them, and the
nation to which they belong, and if this common element.
be faulty or defective in the individual, it is faulty and
defective also in the nation. Hence in considering the
source of defects in the complex organism of a nation
we must descend to classes and to individuals. Reform
the individual, extend the work of reform, and you
roform the nation.

Self-discipline, I have said, delineates the character
of tho individual; therefore it likewise delineates the
character of the nation. It is the moderomen of passion
and feoling, of sentiment and aspiration, and it regu-
lates the standard according to which the nation judges
of events. It moulds the nation’s character and prin-
ciple. Character and principle are co-relative terms,
and 1t is not my province presently to mark the line
oxisting between them; at least they co-exist. It is
principle that makes character, and it is character that
makes the man.

instead of looking on in despair at the increasing im-

Tho arguments of Dr. Kelly are chiofly addressed to

grees take effect if the country will only look seriously
ab its duties instead of blaming the Department for its

It is full time bo claim earnestly from the farmer and

the food produced in his own country instcad of feeding
himself and his live stock so much on foreign raised
and foreign manufactured foods; but it is as well to

cconomic difficulties in the way of a general return to

I here ropeat: —

(1) Irish wheat is said to be undeniably inferior in

Millers will tell one that last year on account of the

could only be u in a smaller proportion,

I imagine a fair answer to this might be that our

departments of living and expenditure, we who are, on
our own showing, the poorest and, certainly, the lowest
wage-earning of the countries we have acquaintance
with, will have nothing but the very best article of
food—the whitest (and therefore least nourishing) flour
and bread, the doarest tea, the .coarsest and least

The one article in which we practice saving and try
a cheaper in preference to a dearer quality is .ba.cqn,
and here I fear I must confess our economy is wise

and delicacy to American, but American is, unfortu-
nately, so good as to be better value in most people’s

the answer often made by householders who are cniti-
cised for solling thoir own pig at 5d. a pound and buy-
ing American bacon at 7d., that tho plentifulnses of
the home-killed pork causes a lavieh consumption of it
while it lasts, and that consequently the samo money’s
worth of bought bacon goes very much farbher. g

If the majority of the people of Ireland could be in-
duced to use wholemeal bread instead of flour bread,
not merely would they be better fed, but the
cultivation of wheat would be enooura.ged, for the
quality of the home-grown grain in meal is fully equal
to that of any imported article.

But in tho moro populous parts of Ircland, tho
western half, brown bread is little used, nor, if we can

about. Certainly tho peoplo of Aran hardly over eab

Indian moal and linseed cake could be easily extended

each district.

Mest of the best feeders are of opinion,  however, that
corn does not give as good a finish to fat cattle as
linseed cake, and it would be hard to maintain that it
could ever pay to grow barley for feeding stuff, that is
for a value of 12s. a barrel. But the average price of
oats, whilo fairly well repaying its cultivation, surely
males it as cheap.a food as Indian corn, and it might,
therefore, be easy to get its use much increased. Prac-
tical lessons on the very plainest cooking for girls in
primary schools seoms tho only means of restoring a
natural and native dietary to our homes. Some good
may also be hoped for from the itinerant courses in
domestic economy carried out by the County Com-
mittees, and from lectures such as those organised by
Most: Roev. Dr. O’Donnell at the Aonach Tirconnaill
this year. Another necessary agency is the cultivation
of National character and self-reliance by the Gaelic
League influence.

But there is no denying the fact that tea, though a
wretched food, is the cheapest and readicst (a considera-
tion in many a “through-other ” house) meal possible.
And it will need long and patient effort to popularise
a healthier and more thrifty and more self-dependent
way of living. The effort is being made in many of
the National schools. When will the rest begin?

That the increased hand-feeding of cattle would in-
crease tillage in order to provide the food is apparent,
but it is not so evident to the farmer that this greater
outlay on cattle feeding would pay. There surely must
be something wrong in our present treatment of grow-
ing cattle when a store beast is generally worth as much
in August as in the following December, and when the
three months’ grass he ate, which has cost about £1
an acre for the period, has gone for nothing. If in-
stead of merely keeping stores alive during the autumn
and winter a system of feeding could add something
each month to their value, and bring them a month
nearer to fattening, in size as well as time, the feeding
ought to be woith its extra cost. But is this practic-
able? Will some successful stock raiser tell us whether
it is, and, if so, how it should be done?

But if graziers are ever to be induced to till some of
their land, and if large and small tillage farmers are to
be able to survive, tillage must be made to pay, and
although strong assertions are made by individuals on
both sides, there is no decided general opinion or
knowledge as to whether tillage is profitable or nct,
and very little effort on the part of landholders to cal-
culate or experiment as to whether it could be made
to pay.

Apart from the common statement of farmers that it
does not we have evidence enough, in the figures of
rent, labour expenses, and of corn and cattle prices,
and in the insolvent condition of the country, that, as
a rule, the present methods of tillage pay very badly.

Wihen will farmers realise that the conditions cannot
bo altered further for at least a generation, and that
the only prospect of improvement, if any, lies in the
alteration of the methods? Rents are now being fixed
finally (and ab the best of a bad bargain) by the Land
Purchase Act sales.  “ Foreign competition,”” which
the farmers are still lamenting over, is as irremediable
a factor in Ireland’s condition as the Cromwellian
Plantation, and as praotical a subject of discussion.
Protection, however desirable in the interests of farm-
ing, would hardly obtain the votes of the Irish towns-
people and labourers even if it was in our power to
ostablish it, but at any rate the country can no more
afford to sit down and wait for it, as some farmers
aro disposed, than it can for the return of Tuatal
Teactmar.

Can we not face the situation as it is and employ
some thought and exertion in trying to make the best
of it? Surely the way to make farming more profitable
is to make it more productive instead of waiting for
impossible circumstances to make its present, amount of
production of more money’s worth.

Can, thon, our prevailing methods of farming—of
tillage and stock raising—be improved? TIf so—how?
